answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe what caused the elbow pain or any injury to the elbow?
answers.intake_a2: I don't know. It started while I was playing tennis.
answers.intake_q3: Can you describe the type of pain you are feeling in your elbow and if it worsens with any specific movements?
answers.intake_a3: it's sharp and hurts with movement
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: icing on the first day and it helped a little
answers.intake_q7: Any difficulty using your elbow for daily activities like gripping, lifting, or bending?
answers.intake_a7: it hurts when I lift things
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms since the elbow pain started?
answers.intake_q9: Any prior history of elbow problems, arthritis, or repetitive strain injuries?
answers.intake_summary: Patient presents with 3 weeks of sharp elbow pain started during tennis, worsened by movement and lifting, without swelling, numbness, or systemic symptoms. No prior elbow issues. Initial icing provided minimal relief.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of sharp elbow pain that began while playing tennis. The pain worsens with movement and lifting objects. She denies any swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, fever, or systemic symptoms. She has no prior history of elbow problems or repetitive strain injuries. Initial treatment with icing provided minimal relief. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by activity-related sharp pain without signs of infection or neurological involvement. 

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Elbow tendinopathy or strain 
3. Early osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Radial tunnel syndrome 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen for pain relief. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure appropriate dosing and duration. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, swelling or redness develops, numbness or weakness occurs, or if systemic symptoms such as fever arise. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks or if diagnosis is unclear.

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…

evidence.title: Broken arm or wrist - NHS
evidence.snippet: n arm or wrist It usually takes around 6 to 8 weeks to recover from a broken arm or wrist. It can take longer if your arm or wrist was severely damaged. You'll need to wear your plaster cast until the broken bone heals. The skin under the cast may be itchy for a few days but this should pass. The hospital will give you an advice sheet on exercises you should…
